text.title: Data Consistency Patterns
text.summary: Saga (choreography vs orchestration), CQRS, Event Sourcing, and Outbox Pattern — the four patterns for managing data consistency across service boundaries without distributed transactions.

rule.statement: Define a compensating transaction for every step in a saga before implementing the forward path.
rule.rationale: A saga without compensating transactions has no rollback. When step 3 of 5 fails, the system is left in a partial state with no path to consistency.

list.item: Choreography: services react to events emitted by peers. Loose coupling, lower complexity, suitable for 3-5 service flows. Risk: hard to trace overall saga state.
Orchestration: central saga coordinator sends commands to services and receives replies. Easier to track state and add timeouts. Use for complex multi-step flows or when visibility is critical.

rule.statement: All compensating transactions must be idempotent; use a correlation ID on every saga step to handle duplicate event delivery.
rule.rationale: Message brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery. A compensation triggered twice must be safe — otherwise rollback itself introduces inconsistency.

section.title: CQRS

rule.statement: Apply CQRS only when the read model and write model have genuinely different optimisation requirements (e.g., read/write ratio above 10:1 or read projections require denormalisation incompatible with the write schema).
rule.rationale: CQRS introduces separate read and write models, event synchronisation, and eventual consistency — significant complexity. For balanced read/write workloads, a single model with read replicas is simpler and sufficient.

section.p: Benefits: 64% average performance improvement for read-intensive operations; independent scaling of read and write paths; separate teams can own each model.

section.title: Event Sourcing

rule.statement: Use Event Sourcing only when the domain requires complete audit trail, temporal queries, or event replay — not as a default persistence strategy.
rule.rationale: Event Sourcing stores ~230% more data than snapshot-based approaches and requires projections (read models) for every query. The complexity is only justified when history itself is a business requirement.

section.p: Benefits: 100% audit trail, temporal queries (state at any point in time), event replay for debugging and new projections. Natural fit for CQRS.

rule.statement: Use the Outbox pattern to guarantee event publication: write to the outbox table in the same database transaction as the business record, then publish via a relay process.
rule.rationale: Publishing to a message broker after committing a database transaction is not atomic: process crash between commit and publish causes lost events. Outbox eliminates this race by making publication a database operation in the same transaction.

list.item: Polling Publisher: relay process queries outbox table periodically and publishes pending messages. Simple; adds latency proportional to polling interval.
Transaction Log Tailing (CDC): relay reads database transaction log (Debezium, AWS DMS). Lower latency; requires database-specific setup.

rule.statement: All Outbox consumers must be idempotent because the relay delivers at-least-once.
rule.rationale: The relay may publish a message multiple times after a crash between publish and outbox row deletion. Consumer idempotency prevents duplicate processing.
